questions.question_text: A Kansas licensee posts a property listing on social media without identifying the responsible broker. What type of violation is this?
distractors: An acceptable informal marketing post
A minor paperwork omission only
A federal Fair Housing Act violation
questions.explanation: Kansas prohibits blind ads, which are advertisements that fail to clearly identify the responsible broker. All advertising, including social media posts, must include the broker's name so consumers know which brokerage is responsible for the listing.

questions.question_text: Agent Rivera creates new business cards featuring only her personal name, photo, and phone number. What is missing to comply with Kansas advertising rules?
distractors: Her license number and expiration date
The KREC official logo and address
A disclaimer about fair housing compliance
questions.explanation: All advertising in Kansas, including business cards, must clearly identify the responsible broker. Agent Rivera's cards are incomplete without the broker's name, which would make them a prohibited blind advertisement under Kansas regulations.

questions.question_text: A real estate team wants to advertise under the name 'Premier Home Group' in Kansas. What must the advertising include?
distractors: Each team member's individual license number
The team leader's personal contact information
A separate KREC team registration number
questions.explanation: In Kansas, team names used in advertising must include the broker's name. Advertising under a team name alone without identifying the responsible broker would constitute a blind ad and violate Kansas advertising regulations.

questions.question_text: Which of the following advertising formats is exempt from the Kansas requirement to identify the responsible broker?
distractors: Internet listings on third-party websites
Yard signs placed on listed properties
Social media posts on personal accounts
questions.explanation: Kansas requires broker identification in all advertising, with no exceptions for format. Business cards, signs, websites, social media, and internet listings must all clearly identify the responsible broker. No advertising medium is exempt from this requirement.

questions.question_text: A broker reviews an agent's new website and finds no mention of the brokerage anywhere on the site. What should the broker require?
questions.correct_answer: Add the broker's name to the website
distractors: Remove the website until KREC approves it
Add only the agent's license number instead
Post a link to the KREC licensee database
questions.explanation: Kansas advertising rules require that all advertising, including websites, clearly identify the responsible broker. The broker should require the agent to add the brokerage name to the website to comply with state regulations and avoid a blind ad violation.

questions.question_text: A Kansas licensee sells a rental property she personally owns, not listed with her broker. What must her advertising include?
questions.correct_answer: A statement that a licensee owns the property
distractors: Her broker's name and office address
A written appraisal from a licensed appraiser
Nothing beyond standard seller disclosures
questions.explanation: When a licensee advertises property she owns that is not listed with her broker, K.S.A. 58-3086(e) requires the advertising to clearly inform the public that a real estate licensee is the owner of or has an interest in the property. Because the property is unlisted, the supervising broker's name is not required under K.S.A. 58-3086(c).

questions.question_text: A Kansas licensee advertises her own unlisted home for sale. What must the advertising clearly disclose?
questions.correct_answer: That a real estate licensee owns the property
distractors: The supervising broker's trade or business name
The listing price and annual property tax amount
That KREC has reviewed and approved the ad
questions.explanation: Under K.S.A. 58-3086(e), advertising of unlisted property owned by a licensee must clearly inform the public that a real estate licensee is the owner of or has an interest in the property; no specific wording is prescribed. Because the home is not listed with a broker, the supervising broker's name is not required under K.S.A. 58-3086(c).

questions.question_text: Agent Park lists his own home on an online marketplace as 'For Sale By Owner' with no mention of his real estate license. What risk does this create?
questions.correct_answer: A violation of owner-licensee disclosure rules
distractors: Automatic revocation of his real estate license
A federal truth-in-lending compliance issue
No risk because the property is not brokerage-listed
questions.explanation: Kansas does not exempt owner-sold property from disclosure: under K.S.A. 58-3086(e), all advertising of unlisted property owned by a licensee must clearly inform the public that a real estate licensee is the owner or has an interest in it. Advertising the home as a plain for-sale-by-owner listing with no licensee disclosure violates the statute and can result in KREC discipline.

questions.question_text: A licensee places a yard sign on her personal property reading 'Home for Sale' with her phone number. She is selling without her broker. What correction is needed?
questions.correct_answer: Add a note that a licensee owns the property
distractors: Replace the sign with the broker's standard sign
Remove the phone number from the sign
Include the county tax parcel number on the sign
questions.explanation: K.S.A. 58-3086(e) requires advertising of unlisted property owned by a licensee to clearly inform the public that a real estate licensee is the owner of or has an interest in the property. Adding a licensee-owner disclosure brings the sign into compliance; the broker's sign is not required because the home is not listed with the broker (K.S.A. 58-3086(c)).

questions.question_text: Why does Kansas require licensees to disclose their status when selling their own property?
questions.correct_answer: To alert buyers they are dealing with a professional
distractors: To ensure the broker receives a commission share
To prevent licensees from owning real property
To satisfy federal mortgage lending regulations
questions.explanation: The owner-licensee disclosure requirement exists to ensure transparency. Buyers should know when the seller holds a real estate license, as that person has professional training and knowledge that may give them a negotiating advantage in the transaction.

questions.question_text: A Kansas supervising broker wants to run the brokerage's primary office out of her home. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, a primary office may be in the broker's residence
distractors: No, a commercial storefront is always required
Only with written approval from KREC each year
Only if no salespersons are affiliated with her
questions.explanation: K.S.A. 58-3060 requires each licensed resident broker to maintain a primary office in Kansas or an adjoining state and expressly allows the primary office to be located in the supervising broker's residence. The office address must be on file with KREC, but Kansas imposes no storefront, signage, or public-access requirements.

questions.question_text: A Kansas broker decides to open a second office location in another city. What must the broker do regarding KREC?
questions.correct_answer: Register the branch office with the commission
distractors: Obtain a separate broker license for that city
Close the original office before opening a new one
Register a new brokerage entity with the state
questions.explanation: Under K.S.A. 58-3060(b), each office other than the primary office is a branch office, and the broker must register it with KREC (branch office application and fee) and designate a branch broker to supervise it. No separate broker license or new brokerage entity is required for an additional location.

questions.question_text: A Kansas broker opens a branch office but names no one to oversee it. Which requirement has been violated?
questions.correct_answer: Designating a branch broker for the office
distractors: Obtaining a second trust account for the branch
Employing at least two salespersons per office
Displaying licenses at the branch location
questions.explanation: K.S.A. 58-3060(b) provides that each office other than the primary office is a branch office and that a branch broker must be designated to supervise it. Kansas repealed its license-display requirement in 2019, a branch office is not required to have its own trust account, and no minimum staffing rule exists.

questions.question_text: Broker Hernandez moves her office to a new address but does not update KREC. Six months later, she opens a branch office without notifying KREC either. How many notification violations have occurred?
distractors: One violation for the branch office only
One combined violation for the brokerage
No violations until the next license renewal
questions.explanation: K.S.A. 58-3060 requires a supervising broker to give KREC written notice within five days of any change in the location of any office, and a new branch office must be registered with the commission when it opens. Each unreported event is a distinct failure, so two separate notification violations have occurred.

questions.question_text: A newly licensed Kansas supervising broker asks where the brokerage's primary office may be located. What does Kansas law allow?
questions.correct_answer: In Kansas or in a state adjoining Kansas
distractors: Only at a commercial storefront in Kansas
Anywhere in the continental United States
Only in the Kansas county where she resides
questions.explanation: K.S.A. 58-3060(a) requires each licensed resident broker to maintain a primary office in Kansas or in an adjoining state, and the office may even be located in the supervising broker's residence. Kansas repealed its old license-display requirement in 2019, so nothing must be posted at the office.
